problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy): develop a data-driven theory of what makes a strong national cybersecurity policy. Three sub-tasks: (1) map global cybercrime distribution and identify disproportionately high-target countries; (2) identify which parts of national policies/laws are effective or ineffective by comparing published policies against cybercrime distribution; (3) correlate national demographics with cybercrime and use that to forecast trends. This O-award paper (control number 2517199) builds the analysis on the VERIS Community Database (VCDB), classifies policies into three families, quantifies their effect on crime trajectories, and forecasts crime with the TimeXer transformer.
models_used: Model I — High-target risk classifier: defines an 'effective cybercrime' count n_eff = n_it·(1 - n_con|it) averaged over 2003–2023, then bins countries into Extreme (>2.0) / High (1.5–2.0) / Moderate (0.5–1.5) / Low (<0.5) risk vs the timeline average

Custom cybercrime risk indicators: Ratio of being found (n_dc/n_it), Ratio of being thwarted (n_con/n_it), Ratio of causing loss (sum X_loss / (n_it - n_suspected)), and Severity (sum X_sig / (n_it - n_overall-rating-NA)), each modeled as Bernoulli indicators X~B(n)

Model II — Policy effectiveness via three-interval gradient & three-interval percentage (slope) analysis: for each policy, compare crime counts across year-before / year-after / +2yr / +3yr, computing absolute gradient (difference) and percentage (ratio) over three intervals; percentage chosen over gradient to neutralize the US's dominating absolute volume

Policy taxonomy model: classified collected national policies into A = institutional development, B = cooperation, C = public education & talent cultivation; averaged across 5 high-crime countries to form a universal 'policy index'

Model III — Correlation screening with three coefficients: Pearson (linear), Spearman rank, and Kendall rank correlation to select demographic predictors and drop weak/missing ones (dropped Network Server for low correlation, Literacy for missing US/UK data)

TimeXer time-series forecaster (improved Transformer with exogenous variables, arXiv 2402.19072): endogenous = monthly cybercrime count via PatchTST non-overlapping patches; exogenous = per-capita GDP, internet penetration, public-education-spend/GDP, higher-education enrollment, embedded iTransformer-style with cross-attention and learnable global tokens
key_techniques: Anchored every claim to a real, citable dataset (VCDB / VERIS framework, ITU GCI, World Bank, UNESCO) rather than synthetic data — a strong fit for a 'data-driven' policy prompt

Engineered domain-specific ratio indicators from raw VERIS fields (Confidence, Impact/Loss, Overall rating) instead of using raw counts, with explicit denominators that subtract 'Suspected' and 'NA' records to remove uncertainty bias

Outlier forensics: traced a June-2023 US spike (751 incidents) to 735 near-identical records differing only in victim Industry/Region, recognized it as one coordinated campaign (not policy-driven), and replaced it with 17 de-duplicated points before correlation/forecasting

Principled method selection by comparison: tested gradient vs percentage analysis and showed percentage avoids US volume domination; compared Pearson/Spearman/Kendall and reasoned about linearity, monotonicity, outlier robustness, and small-sample stability
Ablation study (with vs without exogenous features) reporting MAE 14.313 vs 17.668 and MSE 2.938 vs 3.659 to prove demographic features genuinely improve forecasts

External validation: cross-checked the home-grown risk classifier against the independent ITU GCI and discussed where it agrees (China, Russia, Thailand, Turkey over 2014→2020) and where it diverges (US: high GCI but also high crime)
Monthly-resolution disaggregation with an explicit missing-data redistribution rule (split annual-vs-monthly discrepancy by 12, distribute remainder in fixed order 1,4,7,10,2,5,8,11,3,6,9,12)
Timeline overlay: annotated policy-enactment dates onto per-country crime curves (Canada, UK, India) to motivate the quantitative analysis visually
why_it_won: Judges reward a defensible, end-to-end data-driven narrative on a policy problem, and this paper delivers exactly that. It (1) grounds everything in real authoritative sources (VCDB, GCI, World Bank, UNESCO) so the 'data-driven analysis to support your theory' requirement is met literally; (2) shows judgment at every fork — choosing percentage over gradient, choosing among three correlation coefficients, dropping two weak predictors — rather than applying one method blindly; (3) demonstrates genuine data hygiene with the June-2023 outlier forensics, which signals the team actually understood the dataset rather than just loading a CSV; (4) reaches a concrete, defensible policy conclusion (cooperation-type policies B have the most durable multi-year suppression; all policy types show a delayed effect with >100% in year 1 then <100% in year 2); (5) validates against an independent index (GCI) and is honest about where the model and GCI disagree; and (6) backs the forecasting choice with a clean ablation proving the demographic features earn their place. The use of a 2024 SOTA exogenous-variable transformer (TimeXer) shows technical ambition while staying tied to the policy question.
section_highlights: Summary maps each sub-problem to a named model and points to result figures, so a judge can navigate in seconds. The 'Our Work' four-stage pipeline (Data Preparation → High-Risk Region Location → Policy Evaluation → Demographic Evaluation) gives a clear spine. Strong notation table defining n_it, n_dc, n_con, X_loss, X_sig etc. up front. The policy-effectiveness section is the standout: three bar-chart panels (year1/year2 vs prev, year3 vs year2) read directly into the 'delayed but sustained effect' story and the B-is-most-durable conclusion. The Model Evaluation section is candidly self-critical (model gives only relative not absolute policy scores; cannot fuse country characteristics with specific policies). The required one-page Memo to policymakers is present and reframes the technical findings as plain-language empirical insights (GCI alone is misleading; three policy directions; feature-fusion prediction). Reasoning before every method choice gives the writing a 'we thought about this' tone judges like.
pitfalls_or_limitations: VCDB is voluntary, heavily US/English-biased and sparse — the 'global distribution' and per-country crime counts (often single or low double digits) are far too thin to support country-level policy conclusions; many countries have NA

Tiny sample sizes undermine the forecasts: the TimeXer evaluation effectively predicts only a handful of yearly points (2022, 2023) yet reports MAE/MSE as if validated; using a heavy transformer on ~60 monthly points risks severe overfitting

Policy effectiveness is confounded: crime counts move with global trends, reporting changes, and the dataset's own deduplication practices — the authors note this but still attribute year-2 declines to policy
The 'policy index' averages across only 5 countries and lets one policy event count in multiple categories, so A/B/C effects are entangled and not causally separable

Self-admitted gaps: model outputs only relative (not absolute) policy scores and never fuses specific policies with country-specific characteristics, so it cannot actually predict a given policy's effect in a given country — the core ask
Correlation values are weak (|r| roughly 0.1–0.39) and partly counter-intuitive (Enrollment/Literacy positively correlated with crime); treating these as predictive features is shaky

Stated data ranges are inconsistent (1971–2023, 2003–2023, 2020–2024 appear in different sections), and the rendered figure tables show obvious extraction/duplication artifacts (e.g. repeated 'Malaysia', 'Cambodia', 'Guinea-Bissau' rows)
MSE < MAE in Table VII is internally inconsistent for these magnitudes, suggesting the reported error metrics are not trustworthy
reusable_patterns: For any policy/data prompt, anchor to named authoritative datasets and list them in a Data Source table early — turns 'data-driven' from a buzzword into evidence
Build a small set of normalized, domain-meaningful ratio indicators from raw fields (with denominators that explicitly exclude unknown/NA records) instead of feeding raw counts into models
Always justify a method choice by comparing alternatives on the actual data (gradient vs percentage; Pearson vs Spearman vs Kendall) — show the losing option and why it fails
Include an explicit outlier-forensics paragraph: detect the spike, explain its real-world cause, decide keep/replace, and state the rule — this single move strongly signals data competence to judges
Run a with/without ablation to prove your extra features or complexity earn their keep, and report the error-metric delta
Validate a home-built index against an independent external index (here GCI) and discuss agreement AND divergence rather than hiding mismatches
Structure the report as a 3–4 stage pipeline diagram up front, map each competition sub-question to a named model, and end with a candid Model Evaluation listing what the model cannot do
Use a fixed, stated rule for distributing missing/aggregated data across finer time buckets so monthly disaggregation is reproducible
Reach for a recent exogenous-variable forecaster (e.g. TimeXer) when the prompt asks how external factors drive a target series, and explain the endogenous/exogenous split in plain terms
